children.name: Caprimulgus
children.description: The largest nightjar genus, spanning Africa, Europe, and Asia with about 35 species. Masters of cryptic plumage, they roost motionless on the forest floor or along branches by day, invisible among dead leaves. At dusk they fill the air with churring, trilling, or whistling songs and hawk moths and beetles in a wide rictal-bristled gape.

children.name: Caprimulgus europaeus
children.description: A masterfully camouflaged nocturnal insectivore that lies motionless along a branch by day, its bark-like plumage rendering it almost invisible. At dusk males deliver a sustained mechanical churring over heathlands and forest clearings from Europe to Central Asia. It winters in sub-Saharan Africa, migrating remarkable distances each year.

children.name: Caprimulgus ruficollis
children.description: The largest European nightjar, breeding in the sandy pine forests and scrublands of Iberia and northwest Africa. Its rufous collar and larger size distinguish it from the European Nightjar, which it replaces in much of the western Mediterranean. It winters in West Africa and is strictly nocturnal, hunting large moths and beetles.

children.name: Caprimulgus rufigena
children.description: A widespread African nightjar breeding across the savanna woodlands of southern Africa, where it is one of the most commonly encountered species at night. Its rufous cheek patches are diagnostic in the field and it produces a repeated churring call from the ground or a low perch. It migrates northward within Africa during the austral winter.

children.name: Caprimulgus fraenatus
children.description: A small, dark nightjar restricted to montane and highland areas of East Africa, from Ethiopia south to Tanzania. It inhabits forest edges and moorland, where its dusky, streaked plumage provides excellent camouflage. The species calls with a series of hollow hooting notes quite distinct from other East African congeners.

children.name: Caprimulgus fossii
children.description: One of the most abundant nightjars in sub-Saharan Africa, occurring across a vast range from Senegal east to Ethiopia and south to South Africa. Males lack elongated outer tail feathers, unlike some close relatives, giving the species its name. It favours open woodland, miombo, and thornbush, where it hawks insects above the canopy at night.

children.name: Caprimulgus pectoralis
children.description: Famous for its liquid, musical call — often rendered as 'Good Lord, deliver us' — which rings through African bushveld evenings from Kenya to the Cape. It is one of the most characteristic sounds of southern African game reserves. Its plumage is richly patterned with chestnut, buff, and black, perfectly matching the bark of the trees on which it roosts.

children.name: Caprimulgus poliocephalus
children.description: An Afromontane specialist breeding at elevations from 1,200 to over 3,000 m across highland East Africa and the Ethiopian escarpment. Its grey-headed, streaked appearance and high-pitched trilling song help distinguish it from the lowland nightjars it replaces at altitude. It perches on rocky outcrops and forest paths on moonlit nights.

children.name: Caprimulgus asiaticus
children.description: A common and widespread nightjar of the Indian subcontinent, found from Pakistan east to Vietnam and south to Sri Lanka. Its loud, repetitive 'chuck-chuck-chuck-churr' call is a familiar sound of dry scrub, open woodland, and cultivated land on warm nights. The species rests on bare ground or low branches, relying on camouflage rather than flight to avoid detection.

children.name: Caprimulgus jotaka
children.description: Breeds across a vast area from the eastern Himalayas to Japan and Korea, wintering throughout Southeast Asia and into the Greater Sundas. The grey nightjar is the quintessential East Asian nightjar, occupying both montane forests and lowland woodlands. Its far-carrying 'kurrr' call is a characteristic sound of Asian summer nights.

children.name: Caprimulgus mahrattensis
children.description: A pale, desert-adapted nightjar of the dry scrub and semi-desert of Pakistan and northwest India, named after the British naturalist William Henry Sykes. Its sandy-buff plumage blends perfectly with arid ground, and it is most often detected at night by the glow of its red eye-shine in torchlight. It has very localised wintering areas in the lowlands of western India.

children.name: Caprimulgus affinis
children.description: A highly adaptable nightjar ranging from the Indian plains east through Southeast Asia to the Philippine archipelago and Wallacea. It occupies an unusually wide range of habitats including open woodland, agricultural land, river gravel beds, and even rooftops in cities. Males produce a loud 'chweep' call and display by fluttering white outer tail patches.

children.name: Caprimulgus prigoginei
children.description: One of the least-known nightjars in the world, described only in 1955 from a single specimen collected in the eastern Democratic Republic of Congo by the Belgian ornithologist Alexandre Prigogine. Its exact range, ecology, and population size remain almost entirely unknown. The species is listed as Data Deficient by the IUCN.

children.name: Caprimulgus clarus
children.description: A slim, long-tailed nightjar of arid and semi-arid scrub in East Africa, from Ethiopia south to Tanzania and Mozambique. Its pale, streaked plumage and rather uniform upperparts are distinctive among East African nightjars. It occupies dry thornbush and coastal scrub, and its populations may be partially nomadic in response to rainfall.

children.name: Caprimulgus nigriscapularis
children.description: A forest-associated nightjar of West and Central Africa, distinguished by bold blackish scapular patches that contrast with the otherwise cryptic streaked-brown upperparts. It inhabits rainforest edge, dense secondary growth, and gallery forest up to 1,500 m. The species is poorly studied but appears to be largely sedentary.

children.name: Caprimulgus batesi
children.description: A large, dark nightjar of the lowland rainforests of West and Central Africa, named after the American naturalist George Latimer Bates who collected extensively in Cameroon. It is one of the most poorly known species in the family, recorded from very few specimens, and its vocalizations were only documented in the late twentieth century. It appears to be sedentary in dense primary rainforest.

children.name: Caprimulgus climacurus
children.description: Males of this West African savanna nightjar possess extraordinarily elongated central tail feathers, up to three times body length, used in undulating display flights at dusk. It breeds in the sahel and Guinea savanna belt from Senegal to Sudan, and moves south during the dry season. Despite its modest size, the streaming tail makes it one of the most visually arresting nightjars in Africa.

children.name: Caprimulgus longipennis
children.description: Among the most remarkable birds on the planet, the breeding male develops a single elongated inner primary feather on each wing that grows into a bare shaft tipped with a large webbed 'flag', carried aloft in a slow, moth-like display flight. The standards are shed after breeding. It inhabits woodland and savanna in West and Central Africa.

children.name: Caprimulgus inornatus
children.description: As its name implies, one of the plainer nightjars in Africa, lacking obvious white wing or tail spots and showing relatively uniform sandy-brown plumage. It breeds in the Sahel and dry savannas from Senegambia to Sudan and Ethiopia, and apparently moves south after breeding. Its understated appearance makes it one of the most difficult nightjars to identify in the field.

children.name: Caprimulgus eximius
children.description: A strikingly beautiful Saharan nightjar with warm golden-buff upperparts finely vermiculated with black — some of the most ornate plumage in the family. It occurs in the sandy deserts and stony plains of north-central Africa, from Mauritania and Mali east to Sudan and Chad. Nocturnally active, it roosts on bare sandy ground where it is almost invisible.

children.name: Caprimulgus madagascariensis
children.description: The most widespread nightjar on Madagascar, occurring across the island's western and southern dry forests and open areas. A second subspecies is found on the Comoro Islands. It is strictly nocturnal, detected mainly by its repeated whistling call during warm nights. Its population appears stable but habitat loss in Madagascar's western forests is a growing concern.

children.name: Caprimulgus mandevillei
children.description: A distinctively patterned nightjar of the humid forests of eastern Madagascar, formerly treated as a subspecies of the Madagascar Nightjar but now recognized as a full species based on plumage and vocal differences. It shows a well-defined pale collar separating the dark crown from the streaked back. Its humid forest habitat is under threat from clearance and slash-and-burn agriculture.

children.name: Caprimulgus natalensis
children.description: A medium-sized nightjar of moist woodland, coastal thicket, and wetland margins in East and southern Africa, ranging from Kenya south to eastern South Africa. It is characterized by bold white spots on the outer tail feathers and a loud, repeated calling pattern beginning with sharp notes. It tends to favour denser vegetation than many congeners.

children.name: Caprimulgus stellatus
children.description: A little-known nightjar of dry acacia steppe and thornbush in the Horn of Africa, with a distribution centred on Ethiopia, Somalia, and northern Kenya. It is named for the numerous small white spots that dot its otherwise brown plumage. Very few field observations exist and the species remains poorly understood.

children.name: Caprimulgus ruwenzorii
children.description: A montane nightjar confined to the Albertine Rift highlands of East Africa, including the Rwenzori Mountains for which it is named, as well as the Virunga volcanoes and adjacent ranges. It inhabits bamboo zone, heath, and montane forest edge at elevations between 1,800 and 3,400 m. It is rarely encountered due to its restricted range and poorly known vocalizations.

children.name: Caprimulgus donaldsoni
children.description: A small pale nightjar restricted to the dry lowlands of Somalia, Ethiopia, and northern Kenya, named after the American explorer and naturalist Arthur Donaldson Smith who collected the type specimen in 1895. It inhabits dry Acacia-Commiphora scrub and stony semi-desert. It is rarely encountered in the field and its biology and vocalizations are poorly documented.

children.name: Caprimulgus tristigma
children.description: The most rock-associated nightjar in Africa, favouring bare granite inselbergs, kopjes, and rocky hillsides across a wide range from Guinea east to Ethiopia and south to the Cape. Its entirely dark grey, finely freckled plumage is a superb match for lichen-covered rock surfaces on which it roosts and nests. It calls with a hollow, dog-like barking note.

children.name: Caprimulgus vexillarius
children.description: One of Africa's most spectacular birds: breeding males grow enormously elongated inner primaries that stream behind them in buoyant, flag-waving display flights over woodland. The streamers are shed after the breeding season and the male reverts to a plainer appearance. It breeds in miombo and other broad-leaved woodland in central and southern Africa and migrates to Central Africa in the non-breeding season.

children.name: Caprimulgus atripennis
children.description: A large nightjar of the Indian subcontinent, found in Sri Lanka, peninsular India, and parts of Southeast Asia, named for the British ornithologist Thomas C. Jerdon who described many South Asian birds in the nineteenth century. It favours rocky hillsides, dry deciduous forests, and scrubby terrain. Its deep, resonant churring call is distinctive among South Asian nightjars.

children.name: Caprimulgus solala
children.description: Known to science from a single wing found in 1990 in Nechisar National Park in southern Ethiopia — making it arguably the least-known bird in the world. No complete specimen, photograph, or recording of a living individual was documented for decades. Recent searches have failed to confirm the species, and it remains a tantalizing ornithological mystery.

children.name: Caprimulgus centralasicus
children.description: Known from a single specimen collected in Xinjiang, China, in 1929 and not recorded again with certainty for decades. It is sometimes considered a pale form of the European Nightjar and its validity as a full species remains debated by ornithologists. If valid, it represents a cryptic and highly elusive inhabitant of the arid steppes of Central Asia.

children.name: Caprimulgus ramsayi
children.description: A seldom-observed nightjar of the eastern Himalayas, southern China, and mainland Southeast Asia, inhabiting montane broadleaf forest and scrub at moderate elevations. It is a sombre, darkly marked bird compared with the more patterned congeners it associates with in the region. Its vocalizations and habits are poorly documented in the literature.

children.name: Caprimulgus meesi
children.description: A recently distinguished nightjar of Flores and nearby Lesser Sunda Islands in eastern Indonesia, separated from the Savanna Nightjar complex largely on the basis of vocalisations and morphometric differences. It is named after the Dutch ornithologist Gerlof Fokko Mees, who made major contributions to the knowledge of Indonesian birds. Little is known about its ecology or population status.

children.name: Caprimulgus celebensis
children.description: The resident nightjar of Sulawesi and several adjacent Indonesian islands, occupying a range of habitats from lowland forest edge and scrub to open grassland. It is part of the diverse Asian Caprimulgus radiation and is among the larger species in the region. Its calls are poorly known and field identification in Sulawesi can be challenging.

children.name: Caprimulgus concretus
children.description: A small, dark nightjar restricted to the lowland rainforests of Borneo, Sumatra, and the Malay Peninsula, inhabiting dense primary and tall secondary forest. It is one of the most difficult nightjars to observe in Sundaland and is detected mainly by its distinctive monotonous call on warm tropical nights. Deforestation is a threat across much of its range.

children.name: Caprimulgus monticolus
children.description: A montane nightjar breeding in the Himalayan foothills and the hills of Myanmar and southern China, occupying pine and mixed forest at elevations from 1,000 to 2,500 m. It winters in the lowlands of South and Southeast Asia. Its churring call on cool highland nights is one of the characteristic sounds of Himalayan foothills in summer.

children.name: Caprimulgus indicus
children.description: A medium-large nightjar of hilly forest from the Indian subcontinent east through Indochina and south to the Malay Peninsula. It inhabits dense forest interiors on slopes and ridges, distinguishing it ecologically from the more open-country Indian Nightjar. Its call is a slow, deep churring quite unlike the rapid calls of other South Asian nightjars.

children.name: Caprimulgus nubicus
children.description: A pale, sandy nightjar of arid and semi-arid landscapes from Egypt and the Sudan east through Ethiopia and the Horn of Africa to the Arabian Peninsula. It inhabits dry wadis, acacia scrub, and rocky semi-desert, often near water. Its churring, toad-like call is delivered from rocky ground or low boulders in the heat of African and Arabian nights.

children.name: Caprimulgus aegyptius
children.description: A very pale, sandy nightjar of the Sahara, Arabian deserts, and Central Asian steppes, breeding from North Africa east to Pakistan and wintering in sub-Saharan Africa. Its almost uniform sandy plumage is among the palest of any nightjar, perfectly matched to the pale desert ground on which it rests. It sometimes breeds close to human settlements in desert oases.

children.name: Caprimulgus egresius
children.description: A little-known nightjar endemic to Pemba Island off the coast of Tanzania in the Indian Ocean. It was long treated as a subspecies of the Natal Nightjar before being recognized as a distinct island endemic. Given the restricted area of Pemba Island and ongoing forest loss, its population is considered small and potentially vulnerable.

children.name: Antrostomus
children.description: The New World nightjars formerly lumped in Caprimulgus but now separated based on molecular and morphological evidence. Antrostomus comprises roughly 15 species distributed from Canada south through the Caribbean and Central America to South America, including the iconic Eastern Whip-poor-will. Several species have extremely restricted ranges and are poorly known.

children.name: Antrostomus carolinensis
children.description: The largest nightjar in North America, breeding in open forests and pine-oak woodlands of the southeastern United States and migrating to the Caribbean and Central America in winter. Its loud, repetitive call — 'chuck-will's-widow' — fills warm southern nights and was immortalized in American folk culture. It is large enough to occasionally take small birds and bats in addition to large insects.

children.name: Antrostomus ridgwayi
children.description: A medium-sized nightjar of rocky arid canyons and brushy slopes in northwestern Mexico and extreme southwestern United States, named after the renowned American ornithologist Robert Ridgway. The buff collar is a useful field mark, and its rapid, accelerating call is quite distinct from the other nightjars it co-occurs with in the Sonoran region. It is a bird of dry washes and desert thorn-scrub.

children.name: Antrostomus rufus
children.description: A large, richly rufous nightjar of forests, forest edges, and second growth from Central America south through much of South America east of the Andes. It is one of the most widespread nightjars in the Neotropics and its rolling, repetitive call is a characteristic sound of warm lowland forest nights. Several geographic populations may represent distinct species.

children.name: Antrostomus salvini
children.description: A range-restricted nightjar of northeast Mexico, where it inhabits semi-arid forest and scrub on the Sierra Madre Oriental foothills, named for the British naturalist Osbert Salvin who worked extensively in Central America. It is poorly known and rarely seen; most records come from tape playback surveys at night. Its relationship to adjacent species is not fully resolved.

children.name: Antrostomus badius
children.description: Restricted to the Yucatan Peninsula and adjacent coastal lowlands of Belize and Guatemala, this nightjar inhabits dry deciduous forest and second growth. It was long lumped with the Rufous Nightjar complex but vocal and morphological evidence supports its treatment as a distinct species. Its plaintive call resonates through the humid lowland forest at night.

children.name: Antrostomus sericeus
children.description: A poorly known nightjar of the Amazon Basin, favouring terra firme forest and forest edge in the lowlands east of the Andes from Colombia south to Bolivia and east to northern Brazil. Its white outer tail feathers, which appear silky in the hand, give the species its common name. It is encountered infrequently and details of its breeding biology remain undescribed.

children.name: Antrostomus nigrescens
children.description: A dark, soot-brown nightjar of the Guiana Highlands and adjacent lowlands, occupying rocky outcrops, tepui slopes, and adjacent forest. Its nearly uniform blackish-brown plumage sets it apart from all other South American nightjars and makes it extremely difficult to spot on a dark rock surface. It is often detected by its sharp, high-pitched call.

children.name: Antrostomus vociferus
children.description: One of the most evocative sounds of the eastern North American night, the Eastern Whip-poor-will's insistent 'whip-poor-will' call once filled summer evenings from Canada to the Gulf Coast. Populations have declined sharply in recent decades, linked to insect declines and forest maturation. It migrates to Mexico and Central America in winter.

children.name: Antrostomus arizonae
children.description: Separated from the Eastern Whip-poor-will in 2010 based on vocal and genetic differences, this species replaces it in the pine and pine-oak forests of the Sierra Madre Occidental and the mountains of the American Southwest. Its call is subtly different from its eastern counterpart. It is partially migratory, with some populations descending to lower elevations or moving south in winter.

children.name: Antrostomus noctitherus
children.description: One of the world's most endangered birds, restricted to a small area of dry coastal forest in the southwest corner of Puerto Rico, with a population estimated at fewer than 2,000 individuals. Once thought extinct, it was rediscovered in 1961. It breeds in a very restricted area of karst limestone forest and is threatened by habitat loss and introduced predators.

children.name: Antrostomus cubanensis
children.description: Endemic to Cuba and the Isle of Youth, where it inhabits pine forests, dry deciduous woodland, and coastal scrub. It was long treated as conspecific with the Eastern Whip-poor-will but vocal and genetic analysis confirm it as a distinct species. It is the only resident nightjar breeding on Cuba and its distinctive 'whip-poor-will' variant is part of the island's nocturnal soundscape.

children.name: Antrostomus saturatus
children.description: A dark, understated nightjar of highland forests in Costa Rica and western Panama, formerly treated as a subspecies of the Rufous Nightjar. It inhabits montane oak forest and forest edge between 1,200 and 2,500 m elevation. Its sombre, uniform plumage and limited range make it one of the more elusive nightjars in Central America.

children.name: Antrostomus anthonyi
children.description: A recently described species from dry forest in coastal Ecuador and Peru, separated from the Rufous Nightjar on the basis of vocalizations and morphology. Named for the American collector Alfred Webster Anthony, it inhabits dry deciduous forest in the Tumbesian region — one of the world's most threatened biodiversity hotspots. Its small range warrants careful conservation monitoring.

children.name: Setopagis
children.description: A genus of small to medium South American nightjars characterized by moderately long tails and relatively plain, cryptic plumage. The genus was resurrected when molecular studies confirmed that several species formerly placed in Caprimulgus or Hydropsalis formed a distinct Neotropical lineage. Members inhabit grasslands, forest edge, and riparian scrub across South America.

children.name: Setopagis parvulus
children.description: The smallest nightjar in South America, broadly distributed from Colombia and Venezuela south through the Amazon and into Argentina. Despite its tiny size it is easily located at night by its loud, piercing call repeated persistently from low shrubs or bare ground. It favours open areas, savanna, and forest edges near water.

children.name: Setopagis maculicaudus
children.description: A small nightjar with distinctive white spots on the outer tail feathers, found patchily from southern Mexico south through Central America and into western South America. It inhabits open grassland, savanna, and cerrado, and is often found in areas with scattered scrub and tall grass. Its sharp 'tik-tik-tik' call is more likely to attract attention than its cryptic plumage.

children.name: Setopagis heterurus
children.description: A rarely observed nightjar of the tepui highlands and adjacent lowlands of Venezuela and Guiana, named for the American ornithologist Walter Edmond Clyde Todd who described many South American birds in the early twentieth century. It inhabits open savanna and scrub in the Guiana highlands. Very few records exist and it is considered one of the least-known nightjars in South America.

children.name: Setopagis whitelyi
children.description: A tepui specialist, restricted to the flat-topped sandstone tablelands of the Guiana Highlands, particularly the Gran Sabana area of Venezuela and adjacent Guyana and Brazil, named for the British naturalist Henry Whitely. It inhabits open rocky terrain with sparse vegetation at high elevation. The species is infrequently observed and its behaviour and breeding biology are virtually unknown.

children.name: Setopagis cayennensis
children.description: A common nightjar of open lowland habitats from Trinidad and the Lesser Antilles south through coastal South America to Argentina. Males are strikingly marked with large white tail and wing patches that flash conspicuously in display flights at dusk. It is one of the most frequently encountered nightjars across much of northern South America.

children.name: Nyctidromus
children.description: A small genus of two large-eyed, long-tailed nightjars of the Neotropics and southern North America. They are among the more terrestrial nightjars, often running along roads and forest paths at night with glowing red eye-shine. Males perform elaborate wing-spreading display dances to court females.

children.name: Nyctidromus albicollis
children.description: One of the most abundant and familiar nightjars in the Neotropics, ranging from southern Texas south through Central America and across South America to Argentina. Its loud, liquid 'paur-WHEEE' call is one of the defining sounds of tropical nights across the Americas. It often sits on roads and forest paths and, unlike most nightjars, will run rather than fly when approached.

children.name: Nyctidromus anthonyi
children.description: A recently described species endemic to the Tumbes region of southwestern Ecuador and adjacent Peru, where it inhabits dry deciduous forest and thorn-scrub in a biodiversity hotspot. Named for the American ornithologist Alfred Webster Anthony, it was long confused with the Common Pauraque but is vocally and morphologically distinct. Its small, heavily threatened habitat makes conservation attention a priority.

children.name: Nyctiphrynus
children.description: Four species of small, round-winged, forest-floor nightjars endemic to the forests of Mexico, Central America, and the Pacific coast of Colombia. They are less vocal than many relatives and are seldom recorded away from their favoured dense forest habitats. Several species have very restricted ranges.

children.name: Nyctiphrynus ocellatus
children.description: A small, heavily spotted nightjar of humid lowland forest in the Amazon Basin and foothills of the Andes, where it roosts on the forest floor among leaf litter. The round white spots on its plumage give it a distinctively 'ocellated' appearance quite unlike most other nightjars. It is largely sedentary and generally uncommon throughout its range.

children.name: Nyctiphrynus yucatanicus
children.description: Restricted to the Yucatan Peninsula and Petén region of northern Guatemala, this small nightjar inhabits the dense dry deciduous forest that covers much of the limestone lowlands. It calls with a series of soft whistled notes beginning just after dark. Its small geographic range makes it a target for conservation monitoring.

children.name: Nyctiphrynus rosenbergi
children.description: Restricted to the wet Chocó forests of western Colombia and extreme northwest Ecuador, one of the world's most biodiverse and threatened forest regions. Named for the Dutch naturalist Carl B. von Rosenberg, it is rarely encountered and its vocalizations were not published until modern times. Continued deforestation in the Chocó threatens its limited habitat.

children.name: Nyctiphrynus mcleodii
children.description: A highland specialist of the Sierra Madre Occidental in western Mexico, inhabiting pine-oak forest at elevations of 1,500–2,800 m. It is named for John McLeod, who collected the first specimens in Sinaloa. Distinguishable from the Common Poorwill by its ear-like feather tufts and deeper forest habitat. Its population status is poorly monitored.

children.name: Phalaenoptilus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Common Poorwill, the only bird known to enter extended torpor (hibernation) in winter. Found across western North America in arid and semi-arid scrubland, this small nightjar is notable for its ability to lower its body temperature dramatically and survive weeks without food.

children.name: Phalaenoptilus nuttallii
children.description: The only bird species documented to undergo true hibernation, entering torpor for weeks at a time in rock crevices during cold winters — a behaviour noted by the Hopi people for centuries. Named for the botanist and ornithologist Thomas Nuttall, it is a bird of arid scrub, chaparral, and rocky desert across western North America. Its soft 'poor-will' call carries surprisingly far across the quiet desert night.

children.name: Chordeiles
children.description: The nighthawks — crepuscular to diurnal nightjars that hawk insects in open aerial flight rather than from perches, often seen flying high in flocks near dusk. They have long, pointed wings with a bold white bar and forked tails. Several species are long-distance migrants.

children.name: Chordeiles minor
children.description: One of the most familiar nocturnal birds of North American skies, with a booming dive display and loud 'peent' call familiar over cities and open country alike. It breeds across North America from Canada to Panama and undertakes one of the longest migrations of any nightjar, wintering in South America as far as Argentina. It is a bird of open skies, nesting on flat gravel rooftops as readily as bare forest clearings.

children.name: Chordeiles gundlachii
children.description: Breeds throughout the Caribbean islands from the Bahamas and Cuba south to the Lesser Antilles, named for the German-Cuban naturalist Juan Christoph Gundlach. It winters in South America and is very similar to the Common Nighthawk, distinguished mainly by call. Flocks gather over coastlines and open lowlands on migratory nights.

children.name: Chordeiles pusillus
children.description: The smallest species in the genus, barely larger than a swallow, inhabiting open savanna and cerrado in northern and central South America from Venezuela and the Guianas south to Bolivia and Brazil. It is often found near marshes and rivers, where it hawks small insects at low altitude. Despite its diminutive size, it is lively and aggressive in defense of roost sites.

children.name: Chordeiles rupestris
children.description: Uniquely pale and sandy among the nighthawks, this species is closely tied to large Amazonian rivers where it roosts and nests on sandbars and gravel beaches. It is found from eastern Peru and Bolivia east across the Amazon to the Atlantic coast of Brazil. Flocks of hundreds rest on exposed river islands during the day — an unusual behaviour for the family.

children.name: Chordeiles acutipennis
children.description: Slightly smaller than the Common Nighthawk with a more buffy wing bar, the Lesser Nighthawk breeds from the southwestern United States south through Central America and into South America west of the Andes. It favours desert, scrub, and open arid country, and is often detected by its soft toad-like trilling call heard near dusk over dry washes and grasslands.

children.name: Chordeiles nacunda
children.description: Historically placed here before being moved to Podager, this large, white-bellied nighthawk of open South American grasslands is sometimes still encountered under this name in older literature. It is a highly gregarious species, with flocks of thousands assembling over pampas and savanna at dusk to hawk aerial insects. Its large size and conspicuous white underparts make it unmistakable.

children.name: Podager
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Nacunda Nighthawk, the largest nighthawk in South America. It inhabits open grasslands and savanna from Panama to Argentina and, unlike most nightjars, is active well into daylight hours, soaring high in loose flocks to hawk insects.

children.name: Podager nacunda
children.description: The largest of all nighthawks, conspicuous in open grasslands and cerrado from Panama south to Argentina, where flocks of thousands may gather during migration. Its large size, bold white belly, and broad wings make it unmistakable in the field. Unlike most nightjars it is often active in full daylight, wheeling overhead in loose aggregations above pampas and llanos.

children.name: Lurocalis
children.description: Two species of stocky, short-tailed nightjars that replace most other nightjars in the forest canopy, hunting insects in swift aerial sallies above and just below the canopy rather than from the ground. They have relatively long, pointed wings compared with other nightjars and are fast, agile fliers.

children.name: Lurocalis semitorquatus
children.description: A fast-flying nightjar that forages above the forest canopy and over rivers at dusk, its short, rounded tail and rapid wingbeats distinguishing it from true nighthawks. It ranges from Honduras south through Central America and across tropical South America to Bolivia and southern Brazil. It is most often detected by its loud, distinctive calls as it swoops over the treetops at dusk.

children.name: Lurocalis rufiventris
children.description: A montane replacement for the Short-tailed Nighthawk in the Andes, inhabiting cloud forest edges and montane valleys from Colombia south to Bolivia at elevations between 800 and 2,200 m. Its rufous underparts contrast markedly with the whiter belly of its lowland relative. It also forages in fast aerial sallies above the forest canopy, and its loud whooping call announces its presence at dusk.

children.name: Nyctipolus
children.description: Two species of small South American nightjars recently separated from Caprimulgus on molecular grounds, occupying the rocky outcrops and forest edges of the Guiana region and northeastern Brazil. They are compact birds with short tails and prefer elevated rocky terrain to the forest floor.

children.name: Nyctipolus nigrescens
children.description: A dark, almost sooty nightjar that inhabits granite outcrops, rocky river banks, and adjacent forest edge in the Guiana Shield region of Venezuela, the Guianas, and northeastern Brazil. Its blackish plumage is an almost perfect match for the dark, lichen-covered rock on which it habitually roosts. This species was long confused with other dark nightjars before being separated.

children.name: Nyctipolus hirundinaceus
children.description: A small, swallow-tailed nightjar restricted to the dry caatinga and semi-arid scrub of northeastern Brazil, where it breeds on rocky hillsides and bare ground. Its deeply forked tail is distinctive and gives it an almost swallow-like silhouette in flight. It is considered near-threatened due to the ongoing degradation of caatinga vegetation.

children.name: Hydropsalis
children.description: A South American genus of nightjars notable for the extravagantly elongated outer tail feathers found in males of several species. Molecular studies have resulted in considerable rearrangement of this genus, with some species moved to other genera and others retained. They inhabit forest, grassland, and scrub across South America.

children.name: Hydropsalis torquata
children.description: The male of this species has outer tail feathers that can reach 60 cm — over twice the body length — creating a spectacular scissor shape in flight that has made it one of the most celebrated nightjars in South America. It inhabits open woodland, cerrado, and savanna from eastern Brazil south to Argentina. During the breeding season males display by flying slowly with feathers spread wide.

children.name: Hydropsalis climacocerca
children.description: A riverine nightjar of the Amazon Basin closely associated with large river systems and their sand and gravel islands, where it roosts conspicuously in the open by day. Males have a stepped, ladder-like pattern to the inner tail feathers which gives the species its name. It ranges from Ecuador and Peru east across Amazonian Brazil to the Guianas.

children.name: Hydropsalis forcipata
children.description: A spectacular species in which males possess outer tail feathers even more elongated than those of the Scissor-tailed Nightjar, sometimes exceeding 70 cm, forming a sweeping train behind the bird in flight. It is restricted to the Atlantic Forest region of eastern Brazil, where it inhabits forest edges and clearings at low to moderate elevation. Ongoing deforestation in the Atlantic Forest is a serious conservation concern.

children.name: Hydropsalis brasiliana
children.description: A medium-sized nightjar of the cerrado and Chaco regions of central South America, from eastern Brazil south to Argentina and Uruguay. It lacks the elongated tail streamers of its closest relatives but bears conspicuous white spots on the outer tail. Its call, a repeated 'kraa-kra', is often the only sign of its presence in dense dry scrub at night.

children.name: Hydropsalis anomala
children.description: An unusual nightjar with distinctive strongly recurved outer primaries that give the wing a sickle-like outline in flight. Restricted to grasslands, marshes, and moist campo in the Pampas region of southern Brazil, Uruguay, and northeastern Argentina. It is rarely encountered and its ecology and breeding habits are poorly documented.

children.name: Macropsalis
children.description: A monotypic genus containing one of the most striking nightjars of the South American highlands. The genus name refers to the enormous, forceps-like tail of the male. It inhabits montane grassland and forest edge in the Atlantic Forest region of southeastern Brazil and adjacent Argentina.

children.name: Macropsalis forcipata
children.description: Males of this high-elevation nightjar possess outer tail feathers exceeding the combined body and wing length, creating a streaming 'train' during display flights in open grasslands. It inhabits the campos and forest edges of the southern Atlantic Forest highlands in Brazil and Argentina, typically above 1,000 m. It is declining due to grassland conversion and afforestation with exotic pines.

children.name: Uropsalis
children.description: Two Andean nightjars famed for the extraordinary tail streamers of the males, which are among the longest relative to body size of any bird in the world. They inhabit montane forest edges, rocky Andean slopes, and grass-filled valleys at elevations from 1,500 to 3,500 m.

children.name: Uropsalis segmentata
children.description: The male's deeply forked tail with white-tipped outer feathers creates a swallow-like silhouette as it floats above Andean forest edges and treeline habitats in Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Bolivia. It inhabits cloud forest margins and high-elevation grasslands and is typically detected at dawn and dusk hawking insects above the shrub layer. Females are plain and short-tailed.

children.name: Uropsalis lyra
children.description: Among the most extraordinary tail ornaments in the bird world, the male's outer tail feathers form a sweeping, lyre-like arc that can exceed 60 cm, three times the body length. Found in the Andes from Venezuela south to Bolivia in montane forest and forest edge between 1,000 and 2,800 m. Females are plain brown and unremarkable in comparison.

children.name: Eleothreptus
children.description: A small genus of two South American nightjars with unusual wing shapes — one has sickle-shaped outer primaries and the other has greatly expanded inner primaries in the male. Both are closely associated with wetland edges and open grasslands of the southern cone.

children.name: Eleothreptus anomalus
children.description: A bizarre nightjar whose outer primaries are strongly curved inward, producing a sickle-shaped wing that creates a distinctively fluttering flight style. It inhabits wet grasslands, marshes, and campo limpo in southern Brazil, Uruguay, and northeastern Argentina. It is rarely encountered and is considered vulnerable due to the widespread drainage and agricultural conversion of its wetland grassland habitat.

children.name: Eleothreptus candicans
children.description: Critically endangered and known from only a handful of localities in the cerrado of Brazil and extreme eastern Bolivia, this nightjar is one of the rarest birds in South America. Males are largely white with black-and-brown markings, making them uniquely conspicuous for a nightjar. The species is highly dependent on pristine campo cerrado grassland, most of which has been converted to agriculture.

children.name: Siphonorhis
children.description: Two species of Caribbean nightjars, one of which — the Jamaican Poorwill — was believed extinct for over a century before being rediscovered. Both are small, forest-floor nightjars of island habitats that have suffered greatly from introduced predators and habitat loss.

children.name: Siphonorhis americanus
children.description: Endemic to Jamaica, where it inhabits dry limestone forest and adjacent scrub in the arid southwestern parishes. It was considered extinct for nearly 150 years until rediscovered in 1989 and remains one of Jamaica's rarest birds, threatened by mongoose predation, habitat loss, and road casualties. It produces a haunting 'poo-whee' call and sits motionless on dry leaves.

children.name: Siphonorhis brewsteri
children.description: A tiny nightjar of Hispaniola — shared between Haiti and the Dominican Republic — named for the American ornithologist William Brewster. It inhabits dry thorn-scrub and limestone forest mainly at lower elevations, and its thin, high-pitched call has been compared to an insect. It is considered near-threatened due to continuing habitat loss and is less frequently recorded than the island's other nightjar species.

children.name: Nyctiprogne
children.description: Two small, swift-flying South American nightjars closely associated with large rivers and their sandy margins. They forage at low altitude over water in fast, swallow-like flight and roost on sandbars and rock outcrops in rivers. Nyctiprogne is one of the more aquatically associated nightjar genera.

children.name: Nyctiprogne leucopyga
children.description: A small nightjar broadly distributed across riparian habitats in tropical South America, from Venezuela and Colombia south through the Amazon Basin to Bolivia and central Brazil. Its white rump patch and wing bands flash conspicuously as it skims low over rivers and lakes at dusk. It roosts on exposed sandbars and gravel islands, sometimes in small groups.

children.name: Nyctiprogne viellioti
children.description: A closely related species to the Band-winged Nightjar, formerly considered conspecific but now treated as distinct based on plumage and range differences. Named for the prolific French ornithologist Louis Jean Pierre Vieillot, it inhabits river-edge habitats in the Guiana Shield region and coastal northeastern South America. Like its congener, it forages in swift, low flights over water.

children.name: Eurostopodus
children.description: Seven species of nightjars ranging from South and Southeast Asia through New Guinea and Australia. They tend to be larger than most Caprimulgus species and occupy a variety of habitats from rainforest to open woodland. Several species have elaborate ear-like head tufts and bright eye-shine. The genus is the main nightjar radiation in Australasia.

children.name: Eurostopodus argus
children.description: Australia's most widespread nightjar, occurring across the mainland from the Kimberley to Queensland and south to New South Wales in open woodland, mulga scrub, and spinifex grassland. It is covered in white spots giving it its name. An adaptation unique to this species is its habit of sometimes sheltering eggs and chicks under its wings during the heat of the day.

children.name: Eurostopodus mystacalis
children.description: A large nightjar of eastern Australia and nearby Pacific islands, inhabiting moist eucalypt forest, forest edges, and rainforest margins. It is the largest nightjar in Australia and produces a loud, resonant churring call at night. It also breeds in Papua New Guinea and the Solomon Islands, with Australian birds apparently partially migratory.

children.name: Eurostopodus papuensis
children.description: A large, richly patterned nightjar restricted to the lowland forests and savanna of New Guinea and a few adjacent islands. It is poorly known and its ecology is largely undocumented, though it appears to inhabit the transition zone between dense forest and open woodland. Its calls were not well-described until field recordings became available in the 1990s.

children.name: Eurostopodus macrotis
children.description: The largest nightjar in Asia, notable for prominent ear-like tufts that project above the head and give it a uniquely owl-like appearance among nightjars. It inhabits hilly forest from northeast India east to the Philippines and Sulawesi. Its loud, far-carrying 'tuk-ka-wheee' call is a quintessential sound of Southeast Asian forests at night, and its red eye-shine gleams dramatically in torchlight.

children.name: Eurostopodus temminckii
children.description: A forest nightjar of the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo, named for the Dutch naturalist Coenraad Jacob Temminck. Slightly smaller than the Great Eared Nightjar, it inhabits lowland and hill forest and is detected mainly by its liquid, mellow call on warm tropical nights. It roosts on the forest floor where its cryptic plumage blends with dry leaves.

children.name: Eurostopodus diabolicus
children.description: One of the most enigmatically named birds in the world, the Satanic Nightjar is endemic to the montane forests of northern Sulawesi, Indonesia. It was described from a single specimen in 1931 and not reliably recorded again until the 1990s. Its dark, richly patterned plumage and mysterious nocturnal habits contributed to its striking common name. Very little is known about its ecology.

children.name: Eurostopodus archboldi
children.description: Restricted to the montane grasslands and forest edges of the central highlands of New Guinea, named for the American explorer and naturalist Richard Archbold who led several major expeditions to New Guinea in the 1930s and 1940s. It inhabits high-elevation habitats above 1,500 m and is seldom encountered. Its biology and ecological requirements remain poorly studied.

children.name: Lyncornis
children.description: Three large, eared nightjars of South and Southeast Asia, allied with Eurostopodus but sufficiently distinct to warrant their own genus. They are birds of forest interiors and forest edge in tropical Asia. The Large-tailed Nightjar is the most widespread and best-known species.

children.name: Lyncornis macrotis
children.description: A large nightjar of forest and forest edge from Sri Lanka east through South Asia, Indochina, and the Philippines, with prominent ear tufts that give it an owl-like profile. Its haunting calls on tropical nights have contributed to legends of forest spirits across much of its range. It is among the larger nightjars in Asia and is more commonly heard than seen.

children.name: Lyncornis cerviniceps
children.description: The most widespread and frequently encountered nightjar in Southeast Asia, ranging from northeast India through Myanmar, Thailand, Indochina, and the Malay Peninsula to the Philippines and Sulawesi. A common bird of forest edges, secondary growth, cultivated land, and even well-wooded gardens in cities, it is often the nightjar most people in Asia first encounter. Its repetitive 'chonk' call rings through tropical nights.

children.name: Lyncornis macrurus
children.description: A medium-large nightjar of the Sundaic lowland rainforests of the Malay Peninsula, Borneo, and Sumatra, inhabiting both primary forest and forest edge. Its loud, slightly mournful 'kwok' call is characteristic of Bornean and Sumatran forests at night. Deforestation has reduced its habitat considerably and it is now uncommon across much of its range.

children.name: Gactornis
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Collared Nightjar of Madagascar. It is one of the most distinctive nightjars on the island, characterized by a bold pale collar and large size relative to other Malagasy nightjars. Its affinities lie with the Afro-Asian nightjar radiation.

children.name: Gactornis enarratus
children.description: An enigmatic nightjar restricted to the humid eastern rainforest of Madagascar, where it inhabits forest interior and edge habitats up to 1,800 m. Its broad, pale collar is a striking field mark unique among Madagascar's nightjars, justifying its placement in a separate genus. Deforestation of the eastern rainforest strip is the main threat to this species.
